content:
Fourth-generation vigneron Leanne Puglisi-Gangemi with the award-winning drops. Image supplied

Queensland’s oldest family owned and operated winery has taken home two prestigious accolades at the 34th Cairns Show Wine Awards.

Ballandean Estate Wines was awarded for its 2024 Saperavi with a Gold medal and its 2024 Semillon with a Silver medal.

Held from 17-20 June, the Cairns Show Wine Awards “focus on examining wine quality and style within the context of a tropical location”.

Ballandean Estate said “to shine in such esteemed company” as celebrated producers Bimbadgen, d’Arenberg, De Bortoli and Peter Lehmann “adds another chapter” to its Saperavi reputation especially.

Originating in Georgia, where winemaking dates back more than 8,000 years, the winery said Saperavi has found a second home on the Granite Belt. Ballandean Estate was one of the first to plant the alternative varietal in the region, with vines first established in its Bellevue vineyard in 2008.

The estate’s granite soils reportedly reflect heat onto the fruit, speeding ripening during the day while cool overnight temperatures preserve acidity – resulting in mature, intensely flavoured grapes. The 2024 Saperavi continues this “proud tradition”, having also achieved a 95-point Halliday rating.

Fourth-generation vigneron Leeanne Puglisi-Gangemi shared her delight at the estate’s success.

“We couldn’t be more proud to see our Saperavi and Semillon recognised in the tropical north. Mario and I had the joy of participating at the public tasting, where an impromptu Saperavi masterclass for some of the guests turned into one of the highlights of the event. It’s a thrill to share the story of this remarkable varietal with so many wine lovers.

“We’re also incredibly proud of our chief winemaker Boxi Zhen, who served as a judge for the second year running, lending his refined palate to classes Ballandean Estate wasn’t competing in. It’s a wonderful endorsement of the calibre our young winemaker brings to the national stage.

“The warmth of Far North Queensland’s wine community has been overwhelming, and we’re excited to be in negotiations with a Cairns partner to establish a satellite cellar door – bringing a taste of the Granite Belt to the tropics,” she said.

The 2024 Saperavi and 2024 Semillon will soon be available for tasting at Ballandean Estate’s cellar door and for purchase online from 1 July onwards. The estate said that wine lovers are “warmly invited” to visit the Granite Belt and experience the wines firsthand.

Ballandean Estate said the double win reflects its “enduring commitment to crafting premium cool-climate wines that span both classic and rare varietals”.

This year’s success builds on a strong showing at 2024’s Cairns Show Wine Awards, where winemaker Boxi Zhen’s wines won Gold and the Trophy for the 2024 Semillon Sauvignon Blanc, along with Bronze medals for the 2023 Wild Ferment Viognier, 2023 Shiraz Viognier and 2024 Rosé.

title: Panellists revealed for ‘Sydney Royal 200: The Wine Show Conversation’
summary:
The Royal Agricultural Society of NSW (RAS) has just revealed a new wave of panellists joining the ‘Sydney Royal 200: The Wine Show Conversation’ symposium set for 31 July. The event celebrates 200 years since wine was first included in the Agricultural Society of NSW’s annual show, with the following industry experts set to join the panel discussions: Lauren Hansen, Melanie Chester, Toni Paterson MW, Sally Evans, Rob Diletti and Jack Glover. Former chair of judges, Iain Riggs AM, Samantha Connew, and PJ Charteris, as well as Mike De Iuliis (current chair) have been announced as the panel leads. International judge, Matthew Jukes, who is returning to the Sydney Royal Wine Show after 20 years, will also be involved.

The post Panellists revealed for ‘Sydney Royal 200: The Wine Show Conversation’ appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Industry experts have been unveiled as panellists for the event at the end of July. Image supplied

The Royal Agricultural Society of NSW (RAS) has just revealed a new wave of panellists joining the ‘Sydney Royal 200: The Wine Show Conversation’ symposium set for 31 July.

The event celebrates 200 years since wine was first included in the Agricultural Society of NSW’s annual show, with the following industry experts set to join the panel discussions:

  • Lauren Hansen, owner of Bloomfield Wines: Dux of the Len Evans Tutorial 2024, Young Gun of Wine 2025, Sydney Royal Wine Show judge
  • Melanie Chester, general manager of Giant Steps: 2025 Halliday Winery of the Year and 2026 Pinot Noir of the Year, National Wine Show, Sydney Royal Wine Show panel chair
  • Toni Paterson MW, wine writer and Halliday Tasting Team, chair of judges at 2026 Hunter Valley Wine Show
  • Sally Evans, RAS of NSW board member, Sydney Royal Wine Show committee member (former chair) and Len Evans Tutorial chair
  • Rob Diletti, winemaker at Castle Rock Estate: Sydney Royal Wine Show panel chair, Len Evans Tutorial scholar
  • Jack Glover, marketing & sales at Hill Smith Family Estate, owner of Zephyr Wines, Sydney Royal Wine Show judge

Former chair of judges, Iain Riggs AM, Samantha Connew, and PJ Charteris, as well as Mike De Iuliis (current chair) have been announced as the panel leads. International judge, Matthew Jukes, who is returning to the Sydney Royal Wine Show after 20 years, will also be involved.

Attendees “will have the opportunity to understand how the Australian wine show system works and go behind the scenes to see how wine is assessed”.

A full day of tasting, debate and celebration is planned, reportedly built around four judged brackets of “incredible Australian wine”.

Wines will be tasted blind and scored to showcase how wine is evaluated as part of the show system. There will also be discussion with the expert panels on the history of wine shows, how to get involved and a discussion of how wine shows are evolving and heading forward into the future.

The full-day event is limited to 200 people with 50% of tickets already sold.

The RAS wine show said it has invested over $275,000 in wine scholarships since 2013, providing 63 scholars with the opportunity to advance their understanding of wine assessment.

“This is a chance to gather and understand how wine is assessed as part of the show system, tracing notable moments that have defined the Sydney Royal Wine Show,” said Angys Barnes, chair of the Sydney Royal Wine committee.

“Expect honest conversation, rigorous tasting and a rare behind-the-scenes look at exactly how your wines get assessed when you enter a wine show.”

Tickets include morning tea, lunch, networking drinks, post event canapes and all judging samples. More information is available here.

title: Taste France brings Vin De France to Sydney
summary:
Taste France brings Vin De France to Sydney
As a part of Bastille Festival 2026, Taste France will “transform East Circular Quay into an immersive celebration of Vin De France wines” from 16 to 19 July. Guided tastings and daily masterclasses will be held throughout the four days of the event, with wine cocktails and French wines to be tasted. Organisers said Vin de France “empowers French winemakers to experiment with the unexpected and create wines that are expressive, approachable and full of character”. The presence of French winemakers at the Bastille Festival aims to “break down the barriers of French wine” and encourage festival-goers to enjoy it in an “approachable and affordable way”.

content:

As a part of Bastille Festival 2026, Taste France will “transform East Circular Quay into an immersive celebration of Vin De France wines” from 16 to 19 July.

Guided tastings and daily masterclasses will be held throughout the four days of the event, with wine cocktails and French wines to be tasted.

Organisers said Vin de France “empowers French winemakers to experiment with the unexpected and create wines that are expressive, approachable and full of character”.

The presence of French winemakers at the Bastille Festival aims to “break down the barriers of French wine” and encourage festival-goers to enjoy it in an “approachable and affordable way”.

The Taste France x Vin De France Wine Bar will showcase French food, wine and culture through food markets, live music, French villages and more than 100 exhibitors “celebrating France’s famous joie de vivre”.

Visitors will be able to discover wines from labels such as La Belle Angèle, Tussock Jumper, Louis de Camponac, Vincent Gérard, Roc de l’Abbaye, La Java Rouge and La Petite Perrière.

Masterclasses reportedly offering “a unique opportunity to experience French wine culture through expert guidance, interactive tastings and plenty of delicious surprises along the way” are schedules as follows:

  • 2:30pm | Aromas & Varietals: Taste the Unexpected
  • 4:30pm | Wine & Cheese: The Ultimate French Duo
  • 6:30pm | Cocktail Creation: Mix It Like the French

Duration: 60 minutes

Price: $39 per person

title: Introducing the Halliday Wine Award finalists “doing things differently”
summary:
Introducing the Halliday Wine Award finalists “doing things differently”
The shortlist for the 2027 Halliday Wine Companion Awards has been unveiled, with a particularly “impressive showing” from South Australia’s McLaren Vale region and an influx of micro-producers vying for the top award. To determine the shortlist, the Halliday Tasting Team reviewed 7,520 wines from 1,033 wineries. Notably, 68 wineries submitted for the first time, making it a record-breaking year for new entrants. The shortlist includes six major categories: Winery of the Year, Winemaker of the Year, Viticulturist of the Year, Best New Winery, Best Value Winery, and Dark Horse Winery, with the winning producers set to be announced at an awards ceremony on 19 August.

The post Introducing the Halliday Wine Award finalists “doing things differently” appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Winemakers at Foreign Friends, who have been nominated for Best New Winery. Image supplied

The shortlist for the 2027 Halliday Wine Companion Awards has been unveiled, with a particularly “impressive showing” from South Australia’s McLaren Vale region and an influx of micro-producers vying for the top award.

To determine the shortlist, the Halliday Tasting Team reviewed 7,520 wines from 1,033 wineries. Notably, 68 wineries submitted for the first time, making it a record-breaking year for new entrants.

The shortlist includes six major categories: Winery of the Year, Winemaker of the Year, Viticulturist of the Year, Best New Winery, Best Value Winery, and Dark Horse Winery, with the winning producers set to be announced at an awards ceremony on 19 August.

South Australia leads the nation with the highest number of finalists, including nine shortlisted producers from McLaren Vale, “demonstrating extraordinary quality and regional pedigree”. Victoria’s Yarra Valley follows closely with six finalists, while Tasmania and New South Wales’ Hunter Valley boast five each.

A Queensland winery has even been named finalist for a major award. Granite Belt-based Balancing Heart, established in 2020, has been named a finalist for Dark Horse Winery.

The most submitted variety for the 2027 Halliday Wine Companion Awards was Shiraz, followed by Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, Riesling and Cabernet Sauvignon. Together, these five classic styles accounted for more than half of all submissions.

“We are immensely blessed with the quality and diversity of our Australian wine regions. From Margaret River to McLaren Vale, the Yarra to the Hunter, there are truly world-class bottlings of Chardonnay and Grenache, exciting renditions of Chenin Blanc and Fiano (an inaugural category in this edition), Shiraz in a variety of styles and more being made by producers large and small,” said Halliday’s head of tasting, Katrina Butler.

Despite this strong showing for traditional varieties, Halliday said the shortlist reflects “the dynamic nature of Australia’s current wine culture and evolving tastes”, with around half the list comprising of “small-batch wineries doing things differently”.

Hyper-boutique producers like Bekkers, Sami-Odi, and Koomilya have successfully challenged the larger household-name producers to become finalists for Winery of the Year. Meanwhile, the Best New Winery shortlist is primarily dominated by micro-operations from L’Anima and S. Caica to the tiny 3-hectare Decades and micro-batching trio Foreign Friends.

“Of the 68 new producers reviewed for this edition, many share winery space, fruit, resources, machinery and a beer at the end of the day with the more established, and this collegiate ‘Aussie attitude’ and the sense of community it fosters are palpable. It is a joy to consolidate these entries and present the best of the best,” added Butler.

Barossa Valley’s Sami-Odi, helmed by Fraser McKinley, produces roughly 1,000 cases a year. This cult indie producer relies entirely on a dedicated mailing list, with no cellar door, public tastings or traditional marketing, yet Halliday said wine lovers routinely wait years for the chance to buy a bottle of his unblended, naturally made Shiraz.

Meanwhile, alternative Victorian producer Foreign Friends, run by an all-female trio of sisters Leila and Crystal Davis and friend Juliette Menneteau, is up for Best New Winery. This “innovative label” names each of its individual cuvées after the women and friends who inspire them.

For everyday wine lovers, the Best Value Winery finalists “prove that excellence doesn’t have to break the bank”. Among the most accessible producers on the list are Margaret River’s Evans & Tate and McLaren Vale’s Wirra Wirra, offering exceptional quality with bottles starting from just $17 and $25, respectively.

The finalists were determined by the tasting team consisting of wine critics Dave Brookes, Jane Faulkner, Jeni Port, Marcus Ellis, Mike Bennie, Philip Rich, Shanteh Wale, Toni Paterson MW and head of tasting Katrina Butler. After reviewing wines throughout the year, the judges reportedly conducted a “rigorous” three-day blind tasting of the highest-scoring bottles to determine the ultimate top wines and wineries for each category.

While the tasting team nominates the major awards candidates alongside 18 varietal categories such as Sparkling, Rosé, Red and White Wine of the Year, the Australian public also has its say in the Halliday People’s Choice Award.

Voting for the award is now open here, aiming to “recognise standout cellar doors, exceptional winery restaurants, scenic views and knowledgeable staff”. Submissions close 31 July with the winner to be announced alongside the major awards.

The Halliday Wine Companion 2027 featuring reviews and tasting notes from the judges will be released 20 August for $50 RRP.

Viticulturist of the Year

title: Ten emerging wine professionals awarded bursaries for IMW Symposium
summary:
A new wave of Aussie wine professionals is preparing to step into one of the world’s most influential wine events – the 2027 Institute of Masters of Wine International Symposium (IMW Symposium) in Adelaide. From Margaret River to Orange, ten emerging winemakers, viticulturists, sommeliers, researchers and communicators have been selected out of more than 120 applicants to receive Wine Australia bursaries to attend the event. Over four days in April 2027, the recipients will partake in tastings, discussions and shared experiences alongside international industry leaders and Masters of Wine, “exploring everything from how wine connects with new generations to how regions respond to climate change and sustainability challenges”.

The post Ten emerging wine professionals awarded bursaries for IMW Symposium appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Bursary recipients LTR top row: Cyndal Petty, Emily Spinaze, Elanor Bilogrevic, Michelle Li and Lucy Austen-Paine. LTR bottom row: Rhys Baker, Nuno Frias, Lucy Adam, Natasha Perry and Nina Throsby. Images supplied.

A new wave of Aussie wine professionals is preparing to step into one of the world’s most influential wine events – the 2027 Institute of Masters of Wine International Symposium (IMW Symposium) in Adelaide.

From Margaret River to Orange, ten emerging winemakers, viticulturists, sommeliers, researchers and communicators have been selected out of more than 120 applicants to receive Wine Australia bursaries to attend the event.

The 2027 Emerging Talent bursary recipients are:

  • Cyndal Petty – Wine writer, critic and presenter, Margaret River
  • Eleanor Bilogrevic – Sensory scientist and PhD candidate, AWRI and Adelaide University
  • Emily Spinaze – Winemaker and consultant, Glover Wines, Hunter Valley
  • Lucy Adam – Vineyard operator, Kooyong Wines, Port Phillip Estate, Mornington Peninsula
  • Lucy Austen-Paine – Assistant winemaker, Blue Pyrenees Estate, Pyrenees
  • Michelle Li – Winemaker, Knappstein Wines, Clare Valley
  • Natasha Perry – Cellar hand, Henschke Wines, Barossa Valley
  • Nina Throsby – Group sommelier, Kailis Hospitality Group, Perth
  • Nuno Frias – Portfolio winemaker, Australian Vintage Ltd, Mildura
  • Rhys Baker – General manager, Swinging Bridge Wines, Orange

Over four days in April 2027, the recipients will partake in tastings, discussions and shared experiences alongside international industry leaders and Masters of Wine, “exploring everything from how wine connects with new generations to how regions respond to climate change and sustainability challenges”.

Wine Australia said the bursary removes the barriers to attending while creating a pathway for new voices to take part in conversations that could shape the direction of the industry.

Orange-based bursary recipient Rhys Baker sees the experience as a chance to strengthen the role of regional Australia within those conversations.

“I’m passionate about seeing regional Australian wine represented with confidence on the world stage. I’m looking forward to learning from global leaders in wine and bringing those insights back into the training, tastings and conversations I lead here in Orange.”

South Australian researcher Eleanor Bilogrevic said the opportunity will help connect Australian expertise to global thinking at a critical time for the industry.

“Having these conversations take place here in Australia creates an exciting opportunity to build on our strong contribution to the global conversation shaping the future of wine and wine products. I’m excited to explore new ideas and perspectives that will help inform the next generation of wine research and innovation.”

Emma Symington MW, head of education – EMEA for Wine Australia and IMW Symposium co-chair, was part of the bursary selection panel and said the strength of the applications highlights a confident and capable future for the sector.

“We saw outstanding applications from across the country and across every part of the value chain. It’s incredibly encouraging to see so many early and mid-career professionals ready to engage in global conversations and contribute to the future of Australian wine.

“This cohort brings curiosity, capability and a genuine commitment to sharing what they learn and we’re genuinely excited to see what they bring to the Symposium discussions,” she said.

Across the group, recipients highlighted the opportunity to test assumptions, build international perspective and translate what they learn into practical outcomes, from improving vineyard and winery decision-making to strengthening regional capability, mentoring and knowledge sharing.

“I look forward to having my opinions challenged, refined and expanded as we consider wine within a broader global context,” said Cyndall Petty from Margaret River.

“Having these conversations take place here in Australia creates an exciting opportunity to build on our strong contribution to the global conversation shaping the future of wine and wine products. I’m excited to explore new ideas and perspectives that will help inform the next generation of wine research and innovation,” said South Australia-based Eleanor Bilogrevic.

“This experience will no doubt be eye opening and arm me with knowledge I can bring back to my business and region,” commented Emily Spinaze from the Hunter Valley.

“It is a chance for my current views on the wine industry to be developed, challenged and new ideas to be formed,” noted Lucy Adam from Mornington Peninsula.

“The excitement comes from the anticipation of growth, returning home with new knowledge, fresh perspectives, valuable connections, and experiences with wines I may never have the chance to taste again. It’s a unique opportunity to challenge myself, deepen my understanding of wine, and contribute more meaningfully to the Australian wine community,” stated Victorian Lucy Austen-Paine

“The bursary provides a unique opportunity to learn from global wine leaders, explore the future challenges and opportunities facing our industry. I look forward to bringing those insights back to the Australian wine community and sharing them through my professional and industry networks,” claimed Clare-Valley recipient Michelle Li.

“Receiving this bursary is an incredible opportunity and a significant milestone in my wine career. I’m excited to learn from some of the industry’s leading thinkers and bring those insights back to my region and peers,” said Natasha Perry from the Barossa Valley.

“To attend in Adelaide, learn from industry stalwarts, and build relationships across the global wine community is a genuine privilege. I look forward to bringing what I learn back to Western Australia and contributing to the conversation,” explained Nina Throsby from Perth.

“The wine industry is at a turning point, and I’m excited to be part of the conversations shaping what comes next,” said Mildura-based Nuno Frias.

The bursary covers full Symposium attendance and includes a travel contribution where applicable, “enabling participation regardless of location”.

title: Domaines Jacques Thienpont acquires select Hickinbotham Clarendon Vineyard parcels
summary:
Domaines Jacques Thienpont, the Bordeaux-based family company behind estates including Le Pin in Pomerol, L’If in Saint-Émilion and L’Hêtre in Castillon Côtes de Bordeaux, has acquired select parcels of McLaren Vale’s Hickinbotham Clarendon Vineyard from Jackson Family Wines. The acquisition includes 22 hectares, approximately nine hectares of which are planted to Cabernet Sauvignon, Grenache, Merlot and Shiraz. The vineyard parcels will become part of Pocket Gully, the Adelaide Hills estate established by Domaines Jacques Thienpont in 2024. Fourth-generation winemaker Georges Thienpont said the family was honoured to become stewards of the vineyard parcels.

The post Domaines Jacques Thienpont acquires select Hickinbotham Clarendon Vineyard parcels appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Hickinbotham Clarendon Vineyard parcels (Image supplied)

Domaines Jacques Thienpont, the Bordeaux-based family company behind estates including Le Pin in Pomerol, L’If in Saint-Émilion and L’Hêtre in Castillon Côtes de Bordeaux, has acquired select parcels of McLaren Vale’s Hickinbotham Clarendon Vineyard from Jackson Family Wines.

The acquisition includes 22 hectares, approximately nine hectares of which are planted to Cabernet Sauvignon, Grenache, Merlot and Shiraz. The vineyard parcels will become part of Pocket Gully, the Adelaide Hills estate established by Domaines Jacques Thienpont in 2024.

Fourth-generation winemaker Georges Thienpont said the family was honoured to become stewards of the vineyard parcels.

“Hickinbotham Clarendon Vineyard is an extraordinary site with a rich history,” said Georges Thienpont. “As relative newcomers to the region, we are honoured to become stewards of these select parcels and are committed to preserving their integrity.”

Chris Jackson, co-proprietor of Jackson Family Wines, said the company had confidence in the Thienpont family’s vision for the acquired parcels.

“We have great confidence in the Thienpont family’s vision for these parcels,” said Jackson. “These are exceptional vineyard blocks, and we are pleased to see them entrusted to one of the world’s great wine families. The Thienponts share our commitment to quality and stewardship, and together we look forward to further elevating the reputation of the vineyard on the global stage.”

The Hickinbotham Clarendon Vineyard holds a significant place in McLaren Vale’s history. Planted by the Hickinbotham family in 1971, the vineyard has contributed to wines that have helped define the region’s reputation.

The property has been owned by Jackson Family Wines since 2012 and continues under the leadership of winemaker Chris Carpenter, who maintains the philosophy and precision that have consistently defined the estate.

title: New Wine Australia investment agreement secures AWRI research and industry services to 2030
summary:
Wine Australia and the Australian Wine Research Institute (AWRI) have entered into a new 4.5-year investment agreement from 1 July 2026 to 31 December 2030, to deliver research, development, extension and industry support services for Australia’s grape and wine sector. The agreement continues Wine Australia’s long-standing investment at AWRI, directing industry R&D levies and matching Commonwealth funding to industry-identified priorities. It provides the AWRI with certainty and flexibility, allowing delivery of research and services for the benefit of levy payers.

The post New Wine Australia investment agreement secures AWRI research and industry services to 2030 appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Image courtesy AWRI

Wine Australia and the Australian Wine Research Institute (AWRI) have entered into a new 4.5-year investment agreement from 1 July 2026 to 31 December 2030, to deliver research, development, extension and industry support services for Australia’s grape and wine sector.

The agreement continues Wine Australia’s long-standing investment at AWRI, directing industry R&D levies and matching Commonwealth funding to industry-identified priorities. It provides the AWRI with certainty and flexibility, allowing delivery of research and services for the benefit of levy payers.

Key industry priorities that will be addressed under the new agreement include:

  • product innovation supporting wine quality and style
  • wine process optimisation, technologies and strategies for managing taints, flavour faults and stability risks
  • sustainability, circularity and strategies to address climate change, including reducing inputs
  • support for the Australian grape and wine sector market access strategy, and
  • enabling practice change at scale, delivering the AWRI Helpdesk and information services, and supporting technical grape growing and winemaking capability.

Wine Australia CEO Dr Martin Cole said the agreement aligns levy investment to the sector’s priorities and provides a solid foundation for the continued delivery of research, innovation and services by AWRI.

“This new agreement has been developed collaboratively, with Wine Australia and AWRI management working side-by-side. We are directing investment to the priorities industry set out in the One Grape & Wine Sector Plan to deliver practical outcomes for grape and wine businesses,” Dr Cole said.

“Long-term capability is important and this agreement reflects our shared focus on maintaining essential services industry relies on day-to-day, investing in core grape and wine-focused research, and continuing to build the skills, knowledge and innovation needed to support greater impact for grape and wine businesses into the future.”

Under the agreement – with investment of more than $28 million including industry R&D levies, matching Commonwealth Government contributions and co-investment – AWRI will remain Wine Australia’s largest research partner. Further investment during the term may be supported through the Australian Wine Future Fund.

The agreement also supports continuity of critical sector-facing services that businesses rely on day-to-day, including technical information, regulatory support and market access resources. These services help growers and winemakers make timely decisions and will remain accessible regardless of fluctuations in annual R&D levy revenue, which is linked to crush size.

AWRI Managing Director Dr Mark Krstic said the agreement reflects AWRI’s role in delivering research, technical support and practical tools to the sector.

“AWRI has supported Australian growers and winemakers for more than 70 years and this agreement means we can keep delivering research and services to industry,” Dr Krstic said.

“The AWRI team appreciate the support that Wine Australia provides and are excited about their continued commitment to deliver high priority tangible innovation outcomes. We are focused on providing practical solutions and information that industry can use straight away, while also delivering innovation that supports the sector’s longer-term resilience and sustainability.”

title: WinePro 2026 delivers ‘strong industry engagement’
summary:
Despite ongoing challenges across the wine sector, organisers of the second edition of WinePro NZ say the event has “once again demonstrated the industry's commitment to staying informed, connected and prepared for the future”. Held this week in Blenheim, and closing yesterday, WinePro attracted wine producers, viticulturists, winemakers and industry suppliers from across Australia and New Zealand, all focused on “discovering new technologies, products, services and ideas that can help drive efficiency, innovation and growth”. Organisers say feedback from exhibitors, speakers and visitors has been overwhelmingly positive, reinforcing “the importance of a dedicated event that brings the industry together face-to-face”.

The post WinePro 2026 delivers ‘strong industry engagement’ appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Images courtesy WinePro

Despite ongoing challenges across the wine sector, organisers of the second edition of WinePro NZ say the event has “once again demonstrated the industry’s commitment to staying informed, connected and prepared for the future”.

Held this week in Blenheim, and closing yesterday, WinePro attracted wine producers, viticulturists, winemakers and industry suppliers from across Australia and New Zealand, all focused on “discovering new technologies, products, services and ideas that can help drive efficiency, innovation and growth”.

Organisers say feedback from exhibitors, speakers and visitors has been overwhelmingly positive, reinforcing “the importance of a dedicated event that brings the industry together face-to-face”.

 

“WinePro was never just about today, it was about helping businesses prepare for tomorrow,” said event director Gary Fitz-Roy.

“In a challenging environment, it is encouraging to see so many industry professionals investing time in understanding what’s new, what’s changing and what opportunities lie ahead.”

 

With strong attendance, quality business conversations and a focus on practical solutions for the future, WinePro has continued to establish itself as an important platform for an industry that’s “determined to adapt, innovate and thrive”.

title: Mornington Peninsula wine industry pioneer Nat White remembered
summary:
The Mornington Peninsula wine community is mourning the loss of pioneering winemaker and industry leader Nat White, who passed away peacefully on 20 June 2026 after a long battle with Parkinson's disease. Together with his wife Rosalie, Nat established Main Ridge Estate in 1975, helping lay the foundations for what would become one of Australia's most respected cool-climate wine regions. A founding member of the Mornington Peninsula Vignerons Association and creator of the region's first licensed winery, Nat was renowned not only for his winemaking skill but also for his generosity, mentorship and willingness to support others.

The post Mornington Peninsula wine industry pioneer Nat White remembered appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Image: Nat White in 2011

The Mornington Peninsula wine community is mourning the loss of pioneering winemaker and industry leader Nat White, who passed away peacefully on 20 June 2026 after a long battle with Parkinson’s disease.

Together with his wife Rosalie, Nat established Main Ridge Estate in 1975, helping lay the foundations for what would become one of Australia’s most respected cool-climate wine regions.

A founding member of the Mornington Peninsula Vignerons Association and creator of the region’s first licensed winery, Nat was renowned not only for his winemaking skill but also for his generosity, mentorship and willingness to support others.

Reflecting on a chance meeting with Nat only weeks ago, Montalto Winery founder John Mitchell said: “Throughout our journey Nat and Rose offered their generous support, encouragement and wisdom. Unwavering, loyal, gentle and strong.”

Over five decades, Nat helped guide countless vineyard owners and winemakers, sharing knowledge freely and helping establish the collaborative culture for which the Mornington Peninsula wine community is known today.

Richard McIntyre of Moorooduc Estate recalled seeking Nat’s help when preparing to make his first wines.

“I said, ‘I have no idea how to make wine.’ Nat said, ‘I will hold your hand.’ He did for many years.“

Garry Crittenden of Crittenden Wines described Nat as the region’s “acknowledged technical leader and founding father”.

“Whenever I had a problem, I went straight to Nat — problem solved.”

Martin Spedding of Ten Minutes By Tractor described Nat as “a giant in the story and development of Mornington Peninsula wine“ and credited him with helping create the remarkable collegiate spirit that remains a defining characteristic of the region.

His influence continues through the generations of winemakers he mentored.

“When faced with a difficult winemaking decision, I still find myself thinking, ‘What would Nat do?’ His influence on generations of winemakers is immeasurable,” said current Main Ridge Estate winemaker James Sexton.

 

Image courtesy Mornington Peninsula Wine

 

Rosalie White reflected on the life she and Nat built together at Main Ridge Estate.

“Main Ridge Estate began as Nat’s vague dream of living with the natural world, far removed from traffic chaos, concrete and air-conditioned surround sound. As the vineyard developed, so did our appreciation of the rhythm of life. Spring, a season of promise and new beginnings; Summer, long days of toil; Autumn, a season of fruitfulness; and Winter, a time of mists and quiet peacefulness.

“Nat’s journey has been a joyous one, filled with friends, trials, triumphs, exploration and celebrations.

“Our family is overwhelmed by the hundreds of messages of support and friendship we have already received as we come to terms with the loss of our beautiful man.”

Nat was awarded an Order of Australia Medal (OAM) in 2017 for significant service to the Australian wine industry.

He is survived by Rosalie, their children Dominic and Annalise, and their families.

A private cremation will be held, with a celebration of Nat’s life to be announced at a later date.

title: Wine tasting becomes a spectator sport in a competition worth backing
summary:
Wine-media brand Bottle Shock has opened the 2026 season of its ‘Big Blind League’, a year-long competitive blind-tasting competition run as a sport. The competition is open to any South Australian resident aged 18 and over from trade professionals to total beginners. Better yet – there’s no initial sign-up fee. Bottle Shock described the format as “simple and a little ruthless” with wines being poured into black glasses for players to taste. Bottle Shock founder Brendan Carter explained to Daily Wine News how participants can rack up points. “Anyone who comes to Bottle Shock in Gumeracha and purchases flights ($25) immediately receives five points on the board. If they complete the tasks on those flights, depending on the mission, they can earn an additional 10 points by, for example, ordering items from cheapest to most expensive or identifying the odd one out.” Carter is hoping for the support of venue owners and sponsors to help the project hit the ground running.

The post Wine tasting becomes a spectator sport in a competition worth backing appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The competition aims to help wine channel the same energy as a spectator sport. Image supplied

By Kyra Trussell

Wine-media brand Bottle Shock has opened the 2026 season of its ‘Big Blind League’, a year-long competitive blind-tasting competition run as a sport.

The competition is open to any South Australian resident aged 18 and over from trade professionals to total beginners. Better yet – there’s no initial sign-up fee.

Bottle Shock described the format as “simple and a little ruthless” with wines being poured into black glasses for players to taste.

Bottle Shock founder Brendan Carter explained to Daily Wine News how participants can rack up points.

“Anyone who comes to Bottle Shock in Gumeracha and purchases flights ($25) immediately receives five points on the board. If they complete the tasks on those flights, depending on the mission, they can earn an additional 10 points by, for example, ordering items from cheapest to most expensive or identifying the odd one out.

“At regional cups, points are awarded for participation, which is quite significant. We view the regional cups as major central events where participants can greatly increase their points and ladder standings; they are essentially points super‑events for competitors.

“Those who rank in the regional cups earn points, and the first‑place finisher receives a ‘free run’ into the grand final at the end of the year, eliminating the need to compete further. The total number of points a competitor has determines their final seeding in the grand final,” Carter said.

Providing a few personal details to your palette profile after signing up is a free way to earn five points.

Moreover, regional cups cost $25 to enter and earn participants 15 points each just for showing up. If a participant makes it to the grand final, there are no further costs.

Points climb a live public ladder with the top 24 tasters facing off at a grand final on Saturday 5 December in Gumeracha, where the wines come from anywhere around the world and the last palate standing takes home $5,000.

The League grew from a one-off experiment in 2025 with the same premise called the ‘5k Big Blind’. The 2025 event drew more than 160 entrants over four months, narrowed to a final 24 and ended with a full-day of tasting. The winner was a man named Stefan, a bacteriologist with no wine-industry background, who beat a room of seasoned professionals. Not one taster achieved a perfect score that day.

“Our aim is to show the average punter what wine can do: a smell or taste can transport you to another place. Seeing young people do this on screen helps other young people feel less intimidated when they first approach wine. It isn’t about health claims or pressure to buy; it’s pure curiosity – how can a sip or a sniff take you somewhere else? That’s something wine does better than anything else you could put in your mouth,” Carter shared.

When asked how he came up with the idea, Carter referenced “the late great Australian” Len Evans who created the ‘Options Game’ – a tasting format now used worldwide to teach the flavours of a region – and the historical ‘Judgement of Paris’ blind tasting.

“We wanted to add to that heritage by turning wine tasting into a spectator sport. We’ve combined elements of the ‘Judgment of Paris’ and the ‘Options Game’, first on our YouTube channel and then at the Bottle Shock Bar, where viewers become competitors. Now we’re ready to take the experience on the road, showing that blind‑tasting wine can be both a spectator and a competitive sport.”

Dates are locked in for the regional cups, although Carter said that the venues are still being finalised.

11 July – Clare Cup

22 August – Adelaide Hills Cup

19 September – Barossa Bowl

24 October – McLaren Vale Cup

14 November – Coonawarra Cup

5 December – Grand Final (Bottle Shock, Gumeracha)

Carter is hoping for the support of venue owners and sponsors to help the project hit the ground running.

“All the biggest breakthroughs in the Australian wine industry have come from collaboration rather than competition, so we’re trying something that’s never been done before. We’ve found a way to educate people through entertainment – by turning learning into a sport. Instead of focusing on consumption, we’re gamifying moderation and showcasing the 85% of the wine experience that rarely gets talked about. The remaining 15% is the alcohol, but that’s not what excites us.

“Wine is really about provenance, place and culture. By gamifying the 85%, we hope venues, sponsors and supporters will help us launch this project. It’s a costly, complex endeavour, but it could bring much‑needed hope and joy to an industry that’s increasingly lacking it,” he emphasised.

For individuals looking to support the competition, Carter said that heading to the website and creating a profile is the easiest way.

“Start by visiting the Bottle Shock Bar in Gumeracha, buying a flight and testing your palate. Then head out into our regions and experience how wine reflects the land – the sight, soil, sunshine and the amazing people behind it.

“That’s how you support the competition: share it with your mates. Everyone knows a friend who thinks they’re a bit of a wine person. Invite them to give it a go and cheer them on. It’s not just about the competition; it’s about having a good time watching your mates win or lose – a classic Australian pastime.”

As for what attendees can expect at the cups, Carter flagged “amazing hospitality, plenty of fun, plenty of laughs, amazing wine and fun challenges”.

“At the core of wine lies hospitality and culture. If it isn’t fun and approachable, we’ve missed the very value wine brings to people’s lives.”

title: Meg Brodtmann appointed director of Victorian Wine Show
summary:
The Victorian Wine Show has announced the appointment of Meg Brodtmann MW as its new director, effective 1 July. The organisation said Brodtmann brings “extensive experience in wine industry leadership, winemaking and stakeholder engagement” to the role. It explained she will oversee the show’s operations, judge selection, exhibitor relations and strategic growth initiatives. Victorian Wine Show said that Brodtmann is “well-known in the industry as an accomplished wine professional and Master of Wine with extensive experience across judging, education, consultancy and industry leadership”. Brodtmann said that she is honoured to be joining the show as director at “such an exciting time for the Victorian wine industry”.

The post Meg Brodtmann appointed director of Victorian Wine Show appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Meg Brodtmann will be the new director from 1 July 2026. Image supplied

The Victorian Wine Show has announced the appointment of Meg Brodtmann MW as its new director, effective 1 July.

The organisation said Brodtmann brings “extensive experience in wine industry leadership, winemaking and stakeholder engagement” to the role. It explained she will oversee the show’s operations, judge selection, exhibitor relations and strategic growth initiatives.

Victorian Wine Show said that Brodtmann is “well-known in the industry as an accomplished wine professional and Master of Wine with extensive experience across judging, education, consultancy and industry leadership”.

Brodtmann said that she is honoured to be joining the show as director at “such an exciting time for the Victorian wine industry”.

“The show has a proud history of celebrating the diversity, quality and innovation of our state’s wines, while providing an important benchmark for producers. I look forward to working with the committee, judges, exhibitors and industry stakeholders to build on that legacy, strengthen engagement with our exhibitors and sponsors, and ensure the show continues to evolve and deliver value for winemakers and wine lovers alike,” she said.

She has previously served as an international judge and tasting panel chair, led producer and trade training programs, and delivered masterclasses as well as publications that “have helped elevate understanding of Victoria’s regional diversity and quality”.

“We are excited to welcome Meg, who brings a great depth of knowledge and experience in her technical expertise, educational roles and communication skills. We look forward to seeing the show expand and embrace the changes facing an ever-evolving wine industry under her guidance,” said Narelle King, chair of the Victorian Wine Show.

“We’d like to thank our outgoing director, Tobias Ansted, for all his hard work and efforts in steering the show through a challenging period and look forward to his continued support as a committee member.”

title: “Coonawarra’s best-kept secret” – a month of ‘cosy’ winter experiences
summary:
Coonawarra’s annual Cellar Dwellers celebration will return this July with a month-long program of “rare museum wines, intimate cellar door experiences” and seasonal experiences designed to showcase the region at its most atmospheric”. Coonawarra Vignerons said that this year’s program “offers more than 40 unique reasons to visit”, from long-table dinners to fire-side stargazing. While Coonawarra is internationally recognised for its Cabernet Sauvignon, Cellar Dwellers aims to celebrate the “remarkable ageing potential” of the region's other wines, including Shiraz, Merlot, Chardonnay and Riesling.

The post “Coonawarra’s best-kept secret” – a month of ‘cosy’ winter experiences appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Coonawarra beneath a sea of stars. Image credit Ben Goode Photography

Coonawarra’s annual Cellar Dwellers celebration will return this July with a month-long program of “rare museum wines, intimate cellar door experiences” and seasonal experiences designed to showcase the region at its most atmospheric”.

Coonawarra Vignerons said that this year’s program “offers more than 40 unique reasons to visit”, from long-table dinners to fire-side stargazing.

While Coonawarra is internationally recognised for its Cabernet Sauvignon, Cellar Dwellers aims to celebrate the “remarkable ageing potential” of the region’s other wines, including Shiraz, Merlot, Chardonnay and Riesling.

Heidi Guyett, Coonawarra Vignerons events and marketing officer, said the event continues to evolve while remaining true to its original purpose – celebrating wines that reward patience.

“Cellar Dwellers has always been about championing wines from the cellar and inviting guests to experience and discover just how well Coonawarra’s wines age, when given the time.

“This year we’ve expanded that concept with ‘Winter Embrace’ – inviting guests not only to discover beautifully aged wines, but to immerse themselves in everything that makes winter in Coonawarra so memorable.

“Whether you’re enjoying a museum Cabernet beside a roaring fire, tasting mulled wine in a bubble tent, exploring the stars above the vineyards or sharing stories with the very people who crafted the wines, every experience has been designed to celebrate the season,” she said.

Guyett described winter as “Coonawarra’s best-kept secret” and highlighted the cosy atmosphere that comes with it.

“It’s a chance to slow down, savour the stories in every bottle, and discover what makes Coonawarra so unique. Whether you’re a seasoned wine lover or just starting your journey, there’s something here for everyone.”

Coonawarra Vignerons said that one of the program’s signature events remains the Cellar Dwellers Back Vintage Tasting, which will return on Friday 17 July at Parker Estate.

The walk-around tasting reportedly brings together a dozen Coonawarra producers under one roof, “offering event attendees the rare opportunity to compare museum and back-vintage wines from across the region in a single evening”.

Other event highlights include a dinner catered by resident Chef Darce Kentish (St Bees) as well as Constellations and Cluny presented by Zema Estate.

Coonawarra Vignerons encourages visitors to “make a weekend of it” by exploring the region’s accommodation, cafes, restaurants and heritage attractions.

The Cellar Dwellers Passport promotion also returns throughout July, encouraging visitors to explore multiple cellar doors for the chance to win “premium Coonawarra wine prizes”.

title: Ballandean Estate shines at Cairns Show Wine Awards
summary:
Queensland’s oldest family owned and operated winery has taken home two prestigious accolades at the 34th Cairns Show Wine Awards. Ballandean Estate Wines was awarded for its 2024 Saperavi with a Gold medal and its 2024 Semillon with a Silver medal. Held from 17-20 June, the Cairns Show Wine Awards “focus on examining wine quality and style within the context of a tropical location”. Ballandean Estate said “to shine in such esteemed company” as celebrated producers Bimbadgen, d'Arenberg, De Bortoli and Peter Lehmann “adds another chapter” to its Saperavi reputation especially. Originating in Georgia, where winemaking dates back more than 8,000 years, the winery said Saperavi has found a second home on the Granite Belt. Ballandean Estate was one of the first to plant the alternative varietal in the region, with vines first established in its Bellevue vineyard in 2008. Fourth-generation vigneron Leeanne Puglisi-Gangemi shared her delight at the estate's success.

The post Ballandean Estate shines at Cairns Show Wine Awards appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Fourth-generation vigneron Leanne Puglisi-Gangemi with the award-winning drops. Image supplied

Queensland’s oldest family owned and operated winery has taken home two prestigious accolades at the 34th Cairns Show Wine Awards.

Ballandean Estate Wines was awarded for its 2024 Saperavi with a Gold medal and its 2024 Semillon with a Silver medal.

Held from 17-20 June, the Cairns Show Wine Awards “focus on examining wine quality and style within the context of a tropical location”.

Ballandean Estate said “to shine in such esteemed company” as celebrated producers Bimbadgen, d’Arenberg, De Bortoli and Peter Lehmann “adds another chapter” to its Saperavi reputation especially.

Originating in Georgia, where winemaking dates back more than 8,000 years, the winery said Saperavi has found a second home on the Granite Belt. Ballandean Estate was one of the first to plant the alternative varietal in the region, with vines first established in its Bellevue vineyard in 2008.

The estate’s granite soils reportedly reflect heat onto the fruit, speeding ripening during the day while cool overnight temperatures preserve acidity – resulting in mature, intensely flavoured grapes. The 2024 Saperavi continues this “proud tradition”, having also achieved a 95-point Halliday rating.

Fourth-generation vigneron Leeanne Puglisi-Gangemi shared her delight at the estate’s success.

“We couldn’t be more proud to see our Saperavi and Semillon recognised in the tropical north. Mario and I had the joy of participating at the public tasting, where an impromptu Saperavi masterclass for some of the guests turned into one of the highlights of the event. It’s a thrill to share the story of this remarkable varietal with so many wine lovers.

“We’re also incredibly proud of our chief winemaker Boxi Zhen, who served as a judge for the second year running, lending his refined palate to classes Ballandean Estate wasn’t competing in. It’s a wonderful endorsement of the calibre our young winemaker brings to the national stage.

“The warmth of Far North Queensland’s wine community has been overwhelming, and we’re excited to be in negotiations with a Cairns partner to establish a satellite cellar door – bringing a taste of the Granite Belt to the tropics,” she said.

The 2024 Saperavi and 2024 Semillon will soon be available for tasting at Ballandean Estate’s cellar door and for purchase online from 1 July onwards. The estate said that wine lovers are “warmly invited” to visit the Granite Belt and experience the wines firsthand.

Ballandean Estate said the double win reflects its “enduring commitment to crafting premium cool-climate wines that span both classic and rare varietals”.

This year’s success builds on a strong showing at 2024’s Cairns Show Wine Awards, where winemaker Boxi Zhen’s wines won Gold and the Trophy for the 2024 Semillon Sauvignon Blanc, along with Bronze medals for the 2023 Wild Ferment Viognier, 2023 Shiraz Viognier and 2024 Rosé.

title: Panellists revealed for ‘Sydney Royal 200: The Wine Show Conversation’
summary:
The Royal Agricultural Society of NSW (RAS) has just revealed a new wave of panellists joining the ‘Sydney Royal 200: The Wine Show Conversation’ symposium set for 31 July. The event celebrates 200 years since wine was first included in the Agricultural Society of NSW’s annual show, with the following industry experts set to join the panel discussions: Lauren Hansen, Melanie Chester, Toni Paterson MW, Sally Evans, Rob Diletti and Jack Glover. Former chair of judges, Iain Riggs AM, Samantha Connew, and PJ Charteris, as well as Mike De Iuliis (current chair) have been announced as the panel leads. International judge, Matthew Jukes, who is returning to the Sydney Royal Wine Show after 20 years, will also be involved.

The post Panellists revealed for ‘Sydney Royal 200: The Wine Show Conversation’ appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Industry experts have been unveiled as panellists for the event at the end of July. Image supplied

The Royal Agricultural Society of NSW (RAS) has just revealed a new wave of panellists joining the ‘Sydney Royal 200: The Wine Show Conversation’ symposium set for 31 July.

The event celebrates 200 years since wine was first included in the Agricultural Society of NSW’s annual show, with the following industry experts set to join the panel discussions:

  • Lauren Hansen, owner of Bloomfield Wines: Dux of the Len Evans Tutorial 2024, Young Gun of Wine 2025, Sydney Royal Wine Show judge
  • Melanie Chester, general manager of Giant Steps: 2025 Halliday Winery of the Year and 2026 Pinot Noir of the Year, National Wine Show, Sydney Royal Wine Show panel chair
  • Toni Paterson MW, wine writer and Halliday Tasting Team, chair of judges at 2026 Hunter Valley Wine Show
  • Sally Evans, RAS of NSW board member, Sydney Royal Wine Show committee member (former chair) and Len Evans Tutorial chair
  • Rob Diletti, winemaker at Castle Rock Estate: Sydney Royal Wine Show panel chair, Len Evans Tutorial scholar
  • Jack Glover, marketing & sales at Hill Smith Family Estate, owner of Zephyr Wines, Sydney Royal Wine Show judge

Former chair of judges, Iain Riggs AM, Samantha Connew, and PJ Charteris, as well as Mike De Iuliis (current chair) have been announced as the panel leads. International judge, Matthew Jukes, who is returning to the Sydney Royal Wine Show after 20 years, will also be involved.

Attendees “will have the opportunity to understand how the Australian wine show system works and go behind the scenes to see how wine is assessed”.

A full day of tasting, debate and celebration is planned, reportedly built around four judged brackets of “incredible Australian wine”.

Wines will be tasted blind and scored to showcase how wine is evaluated as part of the show system. There will also be discussion with the expert panels on the history of wine shows, how to get involved and a discussion of how wine shows are evolving and heading forward into the future.

The full-day event is limited to 200 people with 50% of tickets already sold.

The RAS wine show said it has invested over $275,000 in wine scholarships since 2013, providing 63 scholars with the opportunity to advance their understanding of wine assessment.

“This is a chance to gather and understand how wine is assessed as part of the show system, tracing notable moments that have defined the Sydney Royal Wine Show,” said Angys Barnes, chair of the Sydney Royal Wine committee.

“Expect honest conversation, rigorous tasting and a rare behind-the-scenes look at exactly how your wines get assessed when you enter a wine show.”

Tickets include morning tea, lunch, networking drinks, post event canapes and all judging samples. More information is available here.

title: Taste France brings Vin De France to Sydney
summary:
Taste France brings Vin De France to Sydney
As a part of Bastille Festival 2026, Taste France will “transform East Circular Quay into an immersive celebration of Vin De France wines” from 16 to 19 July. Guided tastings and daily masterclasses will be held throughout the four days of the event, with wine cocktails and French wines to be tasted. Organisers said Vin de France “empowers French winemakers to experiment with the unexpected and create wines that are expressive, approachable and full of character”. The presence of French winemakers at the Bastille Festival aims to “break down the barriers of French wine” and encourage festival-goers to enjoy it in an “approachable and affordable way”.

content:

Organisers said Vin de France “empowers French winemakers to experiment with the unexpected and create wines that are expressive, approachable and full of character”.

The presence of French winemakers at the Bastille Festival aims to “break down the barriers of French wine” and encourage festival-goers to enjoy it in an “approachable and affordable way”.

The Taste France x Vin De France Wine Bar will showcase French food, wine and culture through food markets, live music, French villages and more than 100 exhibitors “celebrating France’s famous joie de vivre”.

Visitors will be able to discover wines from labels such as La Belle Angèle, Tussock Jumper, Louis de Camponac, Vincent Gérard, Roc de l’Abbaye, La Java Rouge and La Petite Perrière.

Masterclasses reportedly offering “a unique opportunity to experience French wine culture through expert guidance, interactive tastings and plenty of delicious surprises along the way” are schedules as follows:

  • 2:30pm | Aromas & Varietals: Taste the Unexpected
  • 4:30pm | Wine & Cheese: The Ultimate French Duo
  • 6:30pm | Cocktail Creation: Mix It Like the French

Duration: 60 minutes

Price: $39 per person

title: Introducing the Halliday Wine Award finalists “doing things differently”
summary:
Introducing the Halliday Wine Award finalists “doing things differently”
The shortlist for the 2027 Halliday Wine Companion Awards has been unveiled, with a particularly “impressive showing” from South Australia’s McLaren Vale region and an influx of micro-producers vying for the top award. To determine the shortlist, the Halliday Tasting Team reviewed 7,520 wines from 1,033 wineries. Notably, 68 wineries submitted for the first time, making it a record-breaking year for new entrants. The shortlist includes six major categories: Winery of the Year, Winemaker of the Year, Viticulturist of the Year, Best New Winery, Best Value Winery, and Dark Horse Winery, with the winning producers set to be announced at an awards ceremony on 19 August.

The post Introducing the Halliday Wine Award finalists “doing things differently” appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Winemakers at Foreign Friends, who have been nominated for Best New Winery. Image supplied

The shortlist for the 2027 Halliday Wine Companion Awards has been unveiled, with a particularly “impressive showing” from South Australia’s McLaren Vale region and an influx of micro-producers vying for the top award.

To determine the shortlist, the Halliday Tasting Team reviewed 7,520 wines from 1,033 wineries. Notably, 68 wineries submitted for the first time, making it a record-breaking year for new entrants.

The shortlist includes six major categories: Winery of the Year, Winemaker of the Year, Viticulturist of the Year, Best New Winery, Best Value Winery, and Dark Horse Winery, with the winning producers set to be announced at an awards ceremony on 19 August.

South Australia leads the nation with the highest number of finalists, including nine shortlisted producers from McLaren Vale, “demonstrating extraordinary quality and regional pedigree”. Victoria’s Yarra Valley follows closely with six finalists, while Tasmania and New South Wales’ Hunter Valley boast five each.

A Queensland winery has even been named finalist for a major award. Granite Belt-based Balancing Heart, established in 2020, has been named a finalist for Dark Horse Winery.

The most submitted variety for the 2027 Halliday Wine Companion Awards was Shiraz, followed by Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, Riesling and Cabernet Sauvignon. Together, these five classic styles accounted for more than half of all submissions.

“We are immensely blessed with the quality and diversity of our Australian wine regions. From Margaret River to McLaren Vale, the Yarra to the Hunter, there are truly world-class bottlings of Chardonnay and Grenache, exciting renditions of Chenin Blanc and Fiano (an inaugural category in this edition), Shiraz in a variety of styles and more being made by producers large and small,” said Halliday’s head of tasting, Katrina Butler.

Despite this strong showing for traditional varieties, Halliday said the shortlist reflects “the dynamic nature of Australia’s current wine culture and evolving tastes”, with around half the list comprising of “small-batch wineries doing things differently”.

Hyper-boutique producers like Bekkers, Sami-Odi, and Koomilya have successfully challenged the larger household-name producers to become finalists for Winery of the Year. Meanwhile, the Best New Winery shortlist is primarily dominated by micro-operations from L’Anima and S. Caica to the tiny 3-hectare Decades and micro-batching trio Foreign Friends.

“Of the 68 new producers reviewed for this edition, many share winery space, fruit, resources, machinery and a beer at the end of the day with the more established, and this collegiate ‘Aussie attitude’ and the sense of community it fosters are palpable. It is a joy to consolidate these entries and present the best of the best,” added Butler.

Barossa Valley’s Sami-Odi, helmed by Fraser McKinley, produces roughly 1,000 cases a year. This cult indie producer relies entirely on a dedicated mailing list, with no cellar door, public tastings or traditional marketing, yet Halliday said wine lovers routinely wait years for the chance to buy a bottle of his unblended, naturally made Shiraz.

Meanwhile, alternative Victorian producer Foreign Friends, run by an all-female trio of sisters Leila and Crystal Davis and friend Juliette Menneteau, is up for Best New Winery. This “innovative label” names each of its individual cuvées after the women and friends who inspire them.

For everyday wine lovers, the Best Value Winery finalists “prove that excellence doesn’t have to break the bank”. Among the most accessible producers on the list are Margaret River’s Evans & Tate and McLaren Vale’s Wirra Wirra, offering exceptional quality with bottles starting from just $17 and $25, respectively.

The finalists were determined by the tasting team consisting of wine critics Dave Brookes, Jane Faulkner, Jeni Port, Marcus Ellis, Mike Bennie, Philip Rich, Shanteh Wale, Toni Paterson MW and head of tasting Katrina Butler. After reviewing wines throughout the year, the judges reportedly conducted a “rigorous” three-day blind tasting of the highest-scoring bottles to determine the ultimate top wines and wineries for each category.

While the tasting team nominates the major awards candidates alongside 18 varietal categories such as Sparkling, Rosé, Red and White Wine of the Year, the Australian public also has its say in the Halliday People’s Choice Award.

Voting for the award is now open here, aiming to “recognise standout cellar doors, exceptional winery restaurants, scenic views and knowledgeable staff”. Submissions close 31 July with the winner to be announced alongside the major awards.

The Halliday Wine Companion 2027 featuring reviews and tasting notes from the judges will be released 20 August for $50 RRP.

A full list of finalists is available below:

Winery of the Year

  • Bekkers (McLaren Vale, SA)
  • Forest Hill Vineyard (Great Southern, WA)
  • House of Arras (TAS)
  • Koomilya (McLaren Vale, SA)
  • Oakridge (Yarra Valley, VIC)
  • Sami-Odi (Barossa Valley, SA)
  • Tolpuddle (TAS)
  • Yerringberg (Yarra Valley, VIC)

Winemaker of the Year

  • Adrian Sparks | Mount Pleasant (Hunter Valley, NSW)
  • Drew Tuckwell | Printhie (Orange, NSW)
  • Kerri Thompson | Skillogalee, Wines by KT (Clare Valley, SA)
  • Mark Walpole | Fighting Gully Road (Beechworth, VIC)
  • Michael Downer | Murdoch Hill (Adelaide Hills, SA)
  • Renae Hirsch | Willunga 100 (McLaren Vale, SA)
  • Rohan Smith | Medhurst (Yarra Valley, VIC)
  • Steve Flamsteed | Decades | Salo (TAS and Yarra Valley, VIC)

Viticulturist of the Year

  • Adrian Hoffmann (Barossa Valley, SA)
  • Andrew George, Yarra Yering (Yarra Valley, SA)
  • Ashley Ratcliff, Ricca Terra (Riverland, SA)
  • Ben Thomson, Best’s (Grampians, VIC)
  • Louise Eather | Tyrrells (Hunter Valley, NSW)
  • Richard Wellsmore, Maxwell Wines (McLaren Vale, SA)
  • Ross Pike, Forest Hill Vineyards (Great Southern, WA)

Best Value Winery

  • Evans & Tate (Margaret River, WA)

  • MMAD (McLaren Vale, SA)

Best New Winery

  • Decades (TAS)

title: Ten emerging wine professionals awarded bursaries for IMW Symposium
summary:
A new wave of Aussie wine professionals is preparing to step into one of the world’s most influential wine events – the 2027 Institute of Masters of Wine International Symposium (IMW Symposium) in Adelaide. From Margaret River to Orange, ten emerging winemakers, viticulturists, sommeliers, researchers and communicators have been selected out of more than 120 applicants to receive Wine Australia bursaries to attend the event. Over four days in April 2027, the recipients will partake in tastings, discussions and shared experiences alongside international industry leaders and Masters of Wine, “exploring everything from how wine connects with new generations to how regions respond to climate change and sustainability challenges”.

The post Ten emerging wine professionals awarded bursaries for IMW Symposium appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Bursary recipients LTR top row: Cyndal Petty, Emily Spinaze, Elanor Bilogrevic, Michelle Li and Lucy Austen-Paine. LTR bottom row: Rhys Baker, Nuno Frias, Lucy Adam, Natasha Perry and Nina Throsby. Images supplied.

A new wave of Aussie wine professionals is preparing to step into one of the world’s most influential wine events – the 2027 Institute of Masters of Wine International Symposium (IMW Symposium) in Adelaide.

From Margaret River to Orange, ten emerging winemakers, viticulturists, sommeliers, researchers and communicators have been selected out of more than 120 applicants to receive Wine Australia bursaries to attend the event.

The 2027 Emerging Talent bursary recipients are:

  • Cyndal Petty – Wine writer, critic and presenter, Margaret River
  • Eleanor Bilogrevic – Sensory scientist and PhD candidate, AWRI and Adelaide University
  • Emily Spinaze – Winemaker and consultant, Glover Wines, Hunter Valley
  • Lucy Adam – Vineyard operator, Kooyong Wines, Port Phillip Estate, Mornington Peninsula
  • Lucy Austen-Paine – Assistant winemaker, Blue Pyrenees Estate, Pyrenees
  • Michelle Li – Winemaker, Knappstein Wines, Clare Valley
  • Natasha Perry – Cellar hand, Henschke Wines, Barossa Valley
  • Nina Throsby – Group sommelier, Kailis Hospitality Group, Perth
  • Nuno Frias – Portfolio winemaker, Australian Vintage Ltd, Mildura
  • Rhys Baker – General manager, Swinging Bridge Wines, Orange

Over four days in April 2027, the recipients will partake in tastings, discussions and shared experiences alongside international industry leaders and Masters of Wine, “exploring everything from how wine connects with new generations to how regions respond to climate change and sustainability challenges”.

Wine Australia said the bursary removes the barriers to attending while creating a pathway for new voices to take part in conversations that could shape the direction of the industry.

Orange-based bursary recipient Rhys Baker sees the experience as a chance to strengthen the role of regional Australia within those conversations.

“I’m passionate about seeing regional Australian wine represented with confidence on the world stage. I’m looking forward to learning from global leaders in wine and bringing those insights back into the training, tastings and conversations I lead here in Orange.”

South Australian researcher Eleanor Bilogrevic said the opportunity will help connect Australian expertise to global thinking at a critical time for the industry.

“Having these conversations take place here in Australia creates an exciting opportunity to build on our strong contribution to the global conversation shaping the future of wine and wine products. I’m excited to explore new ideas and perspectives that will help inform the next generation of wine research and innovation.”

Emma Symington MW, head of education – EMEA for Wine Australia and IMW Symposium co-chair, was part of the bursary selection panel and said the strength of the applications highlights a confident and capable future for the sector.

“We saw outstanding applications from across the country and across every part of the value chain. It’s incredibly encouraging to see so many early and mid-career professionals ready to engage in global conversations and contribute to the future of Australian wine.

“This cohort brings curiosity, capability and a genuine commitment to sharing what they learn and we’re genuinely excited to see what they bring to the Symposium discussions,” she said.

Across the group, recipients highlighted the opportunity to test assumptions, build international perspective and translate what they learn into practical outcomes, from improving vineyard and winery decision-making to strengthening regional capability, mentoring and knowledge sharing.

“I look forward to having my opinions challenged, refined and expanded as we consider wine within a broader global context,” said Cyndall Petty from Margaret River.

“Having these conversations take place here in Australia creates an exciting opportunity to build on our strong contribution to the global conversation shaping the future of wine and wine products. I’m excited to explore new ideas and perspectives that will help inform the next generation of wine research and innovation,” said South Australia-based Eleanor Bilogrevic.

“This experience will no doubt be eye opening and arm me with knowledge I can bring back to my business and region,” commented Emily Spinaze from the Hunter Valley.

“It is a chance for my current views on the wine industry to be developed, challenged and new ideas to be formed,” noted Lucy Adam from Mornington Peninsula.

“The excitement comes from the anticipation of growth, returning home with new knowledge, fresh perspectives, valuable connections, and experiences with wines I may never have the chance to taste again. It’s a unique opportunity to challenge myself, deepen my understanding of wine, and contribute more meaningfully to the Australian wine community,” stated Victorian Lucy Austen-Paine

“The bursary provides a unique opportunity to learn from global wine leaders, explore the future challenges and opportunities facing our industry. I look forward to bringing those insights back to the Australian wine community and sharing them through my professional and industry networks,” claimed Clare-Valley recipient Michelle Li.

“Receiving this bursary is an incredible opportunity and a significant milestone in my wine career. I’m excited to learn from some of the industry’s leading thinkers and bring those insights back to my region and peers,” said Natasha Perry from the Barossa Valley.

“To attend in Adelaide, learn from industry stalwarts, and build relationships across the global wine community is a genuine privilege. I look forward to bringing what I learn back to Western Australia and contributing to the conversation,” explained Nina Throsby from Perth.

“The wine industry is at a turning point, and I’m excited to be part of the conversations shaping what comes next,” said Mildura-based Nuno Frias.

The bursary covers full Symposium attendance and includes a travel contribution where applicable, “enabling participation regardless of location”.

summary:
Domaines Jacques Thienpont, the Bordeaux-based family company behind estates including Le Pin in Pomerol, L’If in Saint-Émilion and L’Hêtre in Castillon Côtes de Bordeaux, has acquired select parcels of McLaren Vale’s Hickinbotham Clarendon Vineyard from Jackson Family Wines. The acquisition includes 22 hectares, approximately nine hectares of which are planted to Cabernet Sauvignon, Grenache, Merlot and Shiraz. The vineyard parcels will become part of Pocket Gully, the Adelaide Hills estate established by Domaines Jacques Thienpont in 2024. Fourth-generation winemaker Georges Thienpont said the family was honoured to become stewards of the vineyard parcels.

The post Domaines Jacques Thienpont acquires select Hickinbotham Clarendon Vineyard parcels appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Hickinbotham Clarendon Vineyard parcels (Image supplied)

Domaines Jacques Thienpont, the Bordeaux-based family company behind estates including Le Pin in Pomerol, L’If in Saint-Émilion and L’Hêtre in Castillon Côtes de Bordeaux, has acquired select parcels of McLaren Vale’s Hickinbotham Clarendon Vineyard from Jackson Family Wines.

The acquisition includes 22 hectares, approximately nine hectares of which are planted to Cabernet Sauvignon, Grenache, Merlot and Shiraz. The vineyard parcels will become part of Pocket Gully, the Adelaide Hills estate established by Domaines Jacques Thienpont in 2024.

Fourth-generation winemaker Georges Thienpont said the family was honoured to become stewards of the vineyard parcels.

“Hickinbotham Clarendon Vineyard is an extraordinary site with a rich history,” said Georges Thienpont. “As relative newcomers to the region, we are honoured to become stewards of these select parcels and are committed to preserving their integrity.”

Chris Jackson, co-proprietor of Jackson Family Wines, said the company had confidence in the Thienpont family’s vision for the acquired parcels.

“We have great confidence in the Thienpont family’s vision for these parcels,” said Jackson. “These are exceptional vineyard blocks, and we are pleased to see them entrusted to one of the world’s great wine families. The Thienponts share our commitment to quality and stewardship, and together we look forward to further elevating the reputation of the vineyard on the global stage.”

The Hickinbotham Clarendon Vineyard holds a significant place in McLaren Vale’s history. Planted by the Hickinbotham family in 1971, the vineyard has contributed to wines that have helped define the region’s reputation.

The property has been owned by Jackson Family Wines since 2012 and continues under the leadership of winemaker Chris Carpenter, who maintains the philosophy and precision that have consistently defined the estate.
